content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration · · · Executive/Legal · · · Enforcement · · ·
(916) 322 5660          322 5901          322 6441

Conflict of Interest

322 6444

October 7, 1977

77· 10-267

William P. Hopkins
City Attorney
P.O. Box 3222
Anaheim, CA  92803

Re:  Opinion Request
     No. 77-022

Dear Mr. Hopkins:

Thank you for your letter of August 17, 1977, requesting an opinion with respect to the reporting obligations of Anaheim City Councilmembers pursuant to Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act. This letter is to advise you that the Commission will issue an opinion with respect to legally required participation, the seventh question posed in your letter. A draft opinion will be prepared by the staff for presentation to the Commission and a copy of this draft will be provided to you as soon as possible.

No formal opinion will be issued with respect to the first six questions posed in your letter because they do not raise any substantial questions of interpretation under the Act. Nevertheless, I trust you will find the following informal advice, provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b), to be helpful. Your questions will be answered in the order in which they were posed.

1. and 2.  You ask in questions 1 and 2 whether the recipients of complimentary tickets and annual passes must report such items as gifts on their Statements of Economic Interests if the items are retained and not used, or used only occasionally. The answer is yes. Government Code Section 82028-/ defines "gift" to mean "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." "Payment," in turn, is defined in Section 82044 to mean "a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services

1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

William P. Hopkins
October 7, 1977
Page Two

Of anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible."
Since the complimentary tickets described in your letter
entitle the holder to attend specified events without paying
the admission price charged to other members of the public,
such complimentary tickets constitute items of value. More-
over, since the value of such tickets is in the entitlement
to attend specified events free of charge, the fact that the
holder has not or will not attend the events in question
does not affect the value of the tickets. Thus, the tickets
are "payments" within the meaning of Section 82044, and
since they are complimentary, we can assume that members of
the council have not provided equal or greater consideration
in return for them. Accordingly, the complimentary tickets
constitute gifts which must be reported by each member on
his or her Statement of Economic Interests.

     3. You ask in your third question whether complimentary tickets must be reported as gifts if the recipient
does not return them to the donor, but instead passes them
along as gifts to other persons. Again, the answer is yes.
The Act provides in Section 82030(b)(4) that "[g]ifts which
are not used and which, within 30 days after receipt, are
returned to the donor or delivered to a charitable organization without being claimed as a charitable contribution for
tax purposes" are not reportable on Statements of Economic
Interests. Thus, unless the "other persons" described in
your letter refer to charitable organizations, the complimentary tickets must be disclosed as gifts on the council
member's Statements of Economic Interests.

     4. You ask in your fourth question how complimentary tickets and passes should be valued. Section 81001
provides:

          Whenever in this title the amount of goods,
          services, facilities or anything of value
          other than money is required to be reported,
          the amount shall be the estimated fair market
          value at the time received or expended, and
          a description of the goods, services, or
          facilities shall be appended to the report
          or statement.

In an opinion issued to Controller Kenneth Cory, 1 FPPC
Opinions 153 (No. 75-094-B, Oct. 23, 1975), the Commission
emphasized that the filer's own good faith estimate of the
value of a gift would meet the requirements of the Act. The

William P. Hopkins
October 7, 1977
Page Three


filer need not employ an appraiser or resort to similar
means for determining a gift's value.

Generally speaking, the fair market value of a
gift is the value which the gift would command in the open
market. Thus, the first method of valuation suggested in
your letter clearly is insufficient since it turns on the
actual use of the gift by the holder. As stated earlier,
the value of complimentary tickets is the entitlement to
attend an event rather than the exercise of that right.
With respect to the second method of valuation suggested in
your letter, we agree that looking to the maximum possible
use that could be made of a ticket or pass would yield an
inflated value. We think it is reasonable to assume that
the fair market value of a season pass is less than the
cumulative cost of admission to the event for every day of
the season. Obviously the size of the discount will vary
depending on the event in question.

We think at least three factors should be taken
into account when valuing a complimentary pass:

(1) Can such a pass be purchased on the open
market?

(2) If not, what is the maximum reasonable use a
person might make of such a pass in a year, taking
into account the nature of the event and whether
the pass is transferrable?

(3) If no market price is readily available, what
is a reasonable percentage that a vendor might
discount the price of a pass from the price of
multiple individual tickets in order to induce the
general public to buy a pass?

As mentioned, a filer is entitled to exercise a
measure of discretion when he or she attempts to value a
gift for reporting purposes. A good faith estimate is all
that is required, and it is not necessary for a filer to
consult the Commission or city attorneys such as yourself
for official approval of some particular dollar value prior
to filing a statement. Because the passes and complimentary
tickets you describe are in some cases not easily valued,
however, it may be helpful for us to apply the standards we
have mentioned to a few of the examples your letter poses
for purposes of illustration.

William P. Hopkins
October 7, 1977
Page Four

You indicate that members of the city council
receive six season box seats to the California Angels' base-
ball games. Only the first of the three factors we listed
must be looked to since season tickets for seats similar to
those made available to city council members are sold to the
public and, therefore, have a readily ascertainable market
value. We are informed that a season ticket for a box seat
costs $340 (a slight discount from 81 games times the price
of a single admission, $4.50). Thus, six season tickets
have a fair market value of $2,040.

You indicate also that members of the city council
receive a season pass to Disneyland. We are advised by
officials at Disneyland that such a pass entitles the holder
to four free admissions per use (five admissions if the
holder has three or more children). Each admission is worth
$9.25 ($5.50 admission plus five rides worth $3.75). An
official's children may use the pass only when accompanied
by a parent, although an official may bring as his guest
persons other than his children. The pass is not transferrable.

Disneyland does not sell season passes. In addition,
they advise us that they have never had an occasion to value
a season pass and that they have no idea what one is worth.
Therefore, we must look to the second and third criteria we
have posited to estimate a fair market value. The second
criterion is the maximum use that might reasonably be made
of such a pass. Disneyland advises us that during the first
eight months of 1977, some city officials did not use the
pass at all, others used it as often as once a month, and a
few used it at a rate of twice a month. If maximum reasonable use
were calculated to be 24 times a year, therefore, the total
value of the pass would be $888 (4 admissions multiplied by
$9.25 per admission multiplied by 24 uses).2/ This amount

2/ If statistics are not available to aid in
determining maximum reasonable use, it may be necessary to
simply make an estimate based on one's knowledge of the
nature of the event involved. A movie theater pass, for
example, could reasonably be used as often as once a week
for admission to a single theater, assuming a weekly turnover
of movies, or more if the pass entitles the holder to admission
to more than one theater. The maximum projected use would
be even higher if the pass were transferrable. A pass to
the Movieland Wax Museum, on the other hand, would not be
used nearly as often since the experience from visit to
visit would be relatively constant. It would not be unreasonable
for one to conclude, therefore, assuming the pass is not
transferrable, that the maximum reasonable use of such a
pass would be no more than five or six times a year.

Liam P. Hopkins
October 7, 1977
Page Five


must then be discounted, in accordance with our third criterion,
to determine a fair market value. If 30% ware used as a
reasonable discount figure, the reportable value of the pass
would be approximately $620.

     If a councilmember believes that reporting a gift
of this size might mislead the public because he has never
or only rarely used the gift, he may, of course, include an
explanation on his form. For example, an entry might read:
"Season pass to Disneyland; difficult to value, but value
estimated at $620; never used."

     We wish to stress once again that the reasonable
use and discount figures we used in this example are
by no means the only ones that could be applied to determine
the fair market value of these tickets. We use them only to
illustrate the general approach and standards we think are
appropriate.

     5. In answer to your fifth question, if one returns
a gift to the donor or donates it to a charitable organization,
it would seem prudent, although it is not necessary, for the
official to obtain a copy of a transmittal letter or similar
evidence to document the transaction.

     6. You ask in your sixth question whether the
donor of a complimentary ticket which has a fair market
value of $250 or more is a source of income to the recipient
within the meaning of Section 87103(c). The answer is yes.
Accordingly, the recipient must disqualify himself from
making, or participating in the making of any governmental
decision which would foreseeably and materially affect the
donor in a manner distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally. See Section 87100.

     7. Your last question asks whether the "rule of
necessity" contained in Section 87101 permits the recipient
of a gift worth more than $250 to fully disclose the conflict
involved and then to participate in the decision in order to
prevent a failure of motion by the city council. As mentioned,
the Commission will issue an opinion on this question. In
the meantime, you may rely on the following interim staff
advice provided pursuant to Section 83114(b) of the Act.

     It is the staff's view that the rule of necessity
does not apply to a conflict situation which arises because

William P. Hopkins
October 7, 1977
Page Six

of gifts an official has accepted if it was reasonably foreseeable at the time the gift was received that the official would be asked to vote on a matter affecting the donor. The need for disqualification in these situations, therefore, should be assessed under the standards set forth in Sections 87100 and 87103 without regard to the provisions of Section 87101.

In order to prevent a situation from arising in the future where all or nearly all of the members of the Anaheim City Council are disqualified from acting on a particular matter, we suggest that the members of the city council refrain from accepting gifts worth $250 or more from any donor which may foreseeably be affected by future city council votes.

We understand that our interpretation of "legally required participation" is not one which has heretofore been applied. We also understand that members of the city council may have received gifts of this type in the past and that applying this interpretation of "legally required participation" to past gifts, which could not possibly be returned unused and within 30 days of receipt in accordance with Section 82010(b)(4), would be unfair and would unduly hamper the operations of the city council. We suggest, therefore, that the interpretation of "legally required participation" contained in this letter be applied prospectively only with respect to any gift which a city councilmember returns to its donor promptly following receipt of this letter.

The question, then, is how should conflict situations which arise because of gifts received prior to this letter be handled. We consider it appropriate to apply the standards governing "legally required participation" which we have applied when a gift is not involved. Those standards permit an official to participate in a decision despite a conflict only if a quorum cannot be achieved without that member's participation. If more than one member is disqualified, any reasonable procedure may be used to select the member or members who may participate in the decision. For example, selection might be accomplished by lot. Once a quorum is obtained, however, Section 87101 may not be invoked to permit the participation of any other member. These standards are subject to change since an opinion requesting this question has been accepted by the Commission for a hearing in either November or December. Opinion requested by Matthew L. Hudson, No. 77-007. If the opinion which is finally adopted alters present staff advice, I will let you know.

William P. Hopkins
October 7, 1977
Page Seven


I would add that gifts which have been received in
the past and which are not returned promptly to their donors
following receipt of this letter may still trigger disqualifi-
cation and the rule of necessity may not be invoked to avoid
such a result. The same applies, of course, to gifts worth
$250 or more which are received and retained in the future.

     I hope this answers your questions. If you wish
to appeal the denial of your opinion request with respect to
the first six questions, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have any further questions,
please do not hesitate to contact Michael Baker, Chief of
the Commission's Legal Division.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:MJB:plh

sections.question_synthetic: What are the reporting obligations of Anaheim City Councilmembers under the Political Reform Act regarding gifts, particularly complimentary tickets and passes, including their valuation, disclosure requirements, and conflicts of interest?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Complimentary tickets and passes must be reported as gifts on Statements of Economic Interests regardless of whether they are used, and must be valued at fair market value using good faith estimates based on comparable open market prices or reasonable maximum use discounted by appropriate percentages. Gifts worth $250 or more create sources of income requiring disqualification from related governmental decisions, and the rule of necessity does not apply to foreseeable conflict situations arising from accepted gifts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to seven questions; questions 1-6 receive informal advice under Government Code Section 83114(b), while question 7 regarding the rule of necessity will receive formal opinion. Document contains extensive analysis with statutory citations and specific valuation examples (Angels tickets at $2,040, Disneyland pass at $620).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the reporting obligations of Anaheim City Councilmembers under the Political Reform Act regarding gifts, particularly complimentary tickets and passes, including their valuation, disclosure requirements, and conflicts of interest?

CONCLUSION: Complimentary tickets and passes must be reported as gifts on Statements of Economic Interests regardless of whether they are used, and must be valued at fair market value using good faith estimates based on comparable open market prices or reasonable maximum use discounted by appropriate percentages. Gifts worth $250 or more create sources of income requiring disqualification from related governmental decisions, and the rule of necessity does not apply to foreseeable conflict situations arising from accepted gifts.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Anaheim City Attorney's opinion request regarding reporting obligations for complimentary tickets and passes received by councilmembers, providing detailed guidance on gift reporting, valuation methods, conflict of interest rules, and the application of the necessity exception.
